answer:
In 2026, the Claude Code developer ecosystem has exploded, shifting from scattered configuration files to packaged, shareable plugins and Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. **By May 2026, over 15,134 Claude Code plugin repositories were indexed** [1]. 

Here is a breakdown of where power users find their tools, the most useful specific tools by category, and concrete tactics for building a 2026-grade AI development environment.

### Where Power Users Find Tools
Instead of blindly searching GitHub, power users rely on curated "awesome-lists" and official marketplaces to filter out broken or outdated tools:
*   **hesreallyhim/awesome-claude-code (36.8k stars):** The canonical, hand-curated list of skills, hooks, slash-commands, and agents. Tools that don't work are actively cut [2, 3].
*   **punkpeye/awesome-mcp-servers & wong2/awesome-mcp-servers:** The primary directories for discovering MCP servers to connect external platforms (like Slack, GitHub, or Jira) to your AI [4-6]. 
*   **rohitg00/awesome-claude-code-toolkit:** A comprehensive, single-source toolkit curating 135 agents, 176+ plugins, and multiple MCP configs [7, 8].
*   **The Official Marketplace:** Accessed directly in the CLI via `/plugin marketplace add` or through the Claude Desktop app's visual Discover tab [9].

### Most Useful Claude Code Plugins
Plugins are integrated packages that execute in-process with Claude Code, allowing them to bundle skills, subagents, and hooks together [10].
*   **Frontend Design (829k installs):** Forces Claude to bypass generic "AI slop" interfaces and strictly apply production-grade UX/UI standards. It bans overused fonts and requires bold, distinct aesthetics before writing code [11-13].
*   **Superpowers (752k installs):** A massive multi-agent framework that structures the entire software development lifecycle. It adds capabilities for structured brainstorming, test-driven development (TDD), and subagent delegation [12, 14, 15].
*   **Code-Simplifier (284k installs):** Anthropic’s internal plugin that strictly refactors recently written code for readability (removing duplication and flattening conditionals) **without changing any behavior or logic** [16, 17]. 
*   **Language Server Protocol (LSP) Plugins:** Plugins like **TypeScript LSP** (177k installs) and **Pyright LSP** (91k installs) give Claude Code IDE-like intelligence, allowing it to jump to definitions, view diagnostics, and notice type errors after edits [18, 19].
*   **Security Guidance (175k installs):** Acts as a real-time hook that warns you about unsafe code patterns, command injections, or XSS vulnerabilities exactly when Claude attempts to write them [20].

### Most Useful MCP Servers
MCP servers are out-of-process connectors that allow Claude to dynamically fetch data and execute tools across external services [21].
*   **Context7 (348k installs):** Pulls up-to-date, version-specific documentation directly into Claude's context, preventing the model from hallucinating outdated APIs [22-24].
*   **Zilliz claude-context:** A semantic code search MCP server that indexes massive codebases in a vector database. It utilizes hybrid search (BM25 + dense vector) to pull only relevant code chunks into Claude's context, resulting in a **~40% token reduction** compared to letting Claude scan whole directories [25, 26].
*   **Composio:** A managed MCP gateway that handles secure authentication to over 1,000 external apps. It allows Claude Code to autonomously send emails, create Linear issues, or read Slack incident threads [27-29].
*   **Firecrawl:** Gives Claude reliable web scraping, internet search, and browser automation. It allows your agent to fetch clean markdown from JavaScript-heavy sites or navigate complex pages autonomously [30, 31].

### Top Open-Source / GitHub Tools
*   **Claudebase (by rohithzr):** **A massive lifesaver for environment sync.** It backs up, restores, and syncs your entire Claude Code config (agents, hooks, memory, MCPs, and skills) to a private GitHub repository [32, 33]. It supports multi-machine conflict detection and profile switching (e.g., work vs. personal) [33].
*   **claude-mem (35.9k stars):** A persistent SQLite-backed memory system. It automatically captures everything Claude does, compresses it using AI, and intelligently injects that context into future sessions [34, 35].
*   **getburnd & llm-prices:** Cost-control CLI tools. **Getburnd** reads your local session files to spot token leaks (like verbose tool loops), while **llm-prices** acts as an MCP server allowing Claude to check API costs across 167 models before making expensive requests [36, 37].

### Concrete Tactics for Power Users in 2026
1.  **Stop installing everything; use a minimalist default stack.** Context bloat is the biggest killer of agent sessions. Experts recommend starting with just five plugins: **One LSP** (e.g., TypeScript LSP), **GitHub MCP** (for repo context), **Security Guidance**, **Commit Commands**, and one cross-app workflow tool like **Composio** [12, 38]. 
2.  **Treat awesome-lists as discovery, not installation guides.** Do not bulk-install plugins from lists. Bookmark them, and only search for a tool when you encounter a specific friction point [39]. 
3.  **Sync your brain.** Set up **Claudebase** on day one. When you spend weeks tuning custom subagents and writing `SKILL.md` files, losing your machine or wiping a disk will destroy hundreds of hours of workflow optimization if it isn't synced [33].
4.  **Enforce the 'Caveman' optimization.** If you find yourself burning through tokens, install the **Caveman** skill. It forces Claude to strip out all pleasantries, filler, and narration, keeping only technical facts and code. This routinely cuts output tokens by an average of 65% [40, 41].
5.  **Use the code-simplifier strategically.** Run the `code-simplifier` only at the very end of a long coding session before opening a pull request, rather than on every small change, to avoid burning expensive tokens on continuous reprocessing [17, 42].

claude-mem

Single-file Python CLI that parses Claude Code transcript JSONL and reports cost, prompt-cache hit rate, tool-call distribution, top expensive turns, and actionable optimization recommendations ( --diagnose , --md , --top N ). MIT, no third-party deps.
systemprompt-template

Why it matters: a lot of real engineering work happens outside the editor. Issues are in Linear, incidents are in Slack, specs are in Notion, deploys are in Vercel, and customer or operational data can live in many other systems. Composio is valuable when Claude Code needs to operate across the full workspace.
How to Actually Use Awesome-Lists

Cons: 32 skills is a lot to install if you only need a few. The product-marketing-context setup step is required before most skills produce useful output. Skills are generalist by design, so vertical-specific products (developer tools, enterprise SaaS, consumer apps) will need customization.

The Caveman skill by Julius Brussee makes Claude Code strip narration, filler words, and pleasantries from its responses while keeping every technical fact and code block intact. Benchmarks across 10 real prompts show an average 65% output token reduction (range 22-87%). It includes intensity modes (lite, full, ultra, wenyan), companion skills for commit messages and PR reviews, and a compress sub-skill that rewrites your CLAUDE.md to cut input tokens by ~46% per session.
Cons: Requires reprocessing code that was already token-expensive to generate. Run it selectively on genuinely complex output rather than as a routine pass on every small change.
